steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence uses em-dashes to set off a parenthetical: "including the ______ Javanese and Bikol, which are spoken by 100 million and 4.6 million speakers, respectively." The blank follows "the" and precedes the specific language names. The question is whether any punctuation should appear after the word "languages" before those names.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘languages’: No punctuation after "languages" lets the names directly identify the noun, the same way "the planets Mars and Venus" needs no punctuation. Clean and grammatical inside the em-dash parenthetical. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘languages:’: A colon introduces a list or explanation at the end of an independent clause. "Including the languages:" is not an independent clause, and a colon inside an em-dash parenthetical is a double-punctuation error. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘languages—’: An em-dash here would open a second nested parenthetical inside the one already opened by the em-dash before "including." That creates an unclosed em-dash pair with no matching close. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘languages,’: A comma after "languages" would make "Javanese and Bikol" a nonessential appositive, implying the names are just extra information rather than the specific items being identified. That misrepresents the sentence's meaning. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
